steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student's claim has two parts: people have (1) mixed feelings about cultured meat that (2) may be a barrier to adoption. The supporting quotation must show both — ambivalence toward cultured meat specifically, not just positive or negative views, and something that would actually block people from embracing it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Many consumers who were surveyed believed that cultured meat would be good for the environment if widely available, but few of those same consumers were willing to try eating cultured meat themselves.": shows consumers holding conflicting views — positive belief but behavioral reluctance — which is exactly the "mixed feelings" that would be a barrier to adoption. ✓
  • Choice B — "Consumers tend to believe that using less packaging when selling meat products in stores would have a significant effect on the environment.": concerns packaging for conventional meat, not cultured meat at all — irrelevant to the claim. ✗
  • Choice C — "Advocates of lab-grown meat claim that it's safer than conventional meat because the means of its production can be tightly controlled.": presents only the positive side from advocates, not mixed feelings from consumers. ✗
  • Choice D — "The flavor of meat from livestock differs across species (from pig to chicken to cow), and is also influenced by farming conditions and the breeds and genders of animals.": describes variation in conventional livestock flavor — no connection to consumer attitudes about cultured meat. ✗
A is correct.
